Case title: REPRO-Bench agentic reproducibility assessment of social-science papers
Short description: Benchmark whether agents can assess computational reproducibility by aligning paper claims with code/data packages and reproduction reports.
Empirical unit of analysis: Paper PDFs, reproduction packages, and reproducibility scores
Candidate roles tested: Protocol and provenance architect; accountability and labor allocator
Key typology question: Is claim-level reproducibility assessment a durable human role even when execution can be automated?
Observable artifacts / traces: Assessment traces, reproduced outputs, claim-alignment judgments, error classifications
Key outcomes observed: Assessment accuracy, failure modes, claim-output alignment, labor saved
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Agents match human reproducibility judgments on real social-science packages
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Benchmark tasks still provide agents with unusually convenient access to code and data
Why this case has high leverage: It separates rerunning code from adjudicating whether a paper's claims are actually supported.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
